title: AI: Workflows
subtitle: A short, no-fluff newsletter for curious minds
lede: Workflows are the part of AI where the engineering happens. Above the model, below the user, between the chat completion and the production system that's actually doing the work. They are the chassis around the engine—the steps, the handoffs, the retries, the verifications, the humans you ask and the humans you don't. In 2026, the workflow layer is where the money is being raised, the bills are being run up, and the bugs are getting shipped. And almost every "novel" pattern in it has a published ancestor between 1972 and 2014.

sections.heading: The simple picture
sections.body:
Anthropic's December 2024 essay drew the line and the rest of the industry quietly accepted it. A workflow is a system where the model is steered through predefined code paths—chain, route, parallelize, evaluate, refine. An agent is a system where the model steers itself, picking tools and steps at runtime. One is a kitchen with prep stations laid out in advance. The other is a chef who walks in, looks at the inventory, and decides what to make.

The cut matters because everything else flows from it. If your task is "summarize this PDF, draft a Slack message, send it" you want a workflow—three stations, no improvisation. If your task is "deal with my inbox today" you want an agent. Most production systems shipping in 2025–2026 are workflows that contain agents at specific stations. The chef gets one prep station. The other five are predefined.

sections.heading: Why everyone shipped one
sections.body:
The funding tells the story faster than the engineering does. Temporal raised $300M Series D at a $5B valuation in February. CrewAI raised $18M. Mastra is at $35M total with twenty-two thousand GitHub stars. n8n closed a $180M Series C last October. LangGraph counts Replit, Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna, and J.P. Morgan as customers. Sierra is doing $150M ARR and was valued at $15.8B in May. Decagon is at $4.5B. Anthropic shipped Claude Code Dynamic Workflows on May 28, which is how Bun turned roughly 750,000 lines of Zig into Rust at 99.8% test-pass in eleven days.

The boom is real. It is also, per Gartner, going to lose forty percent of its projects to the trash heap by 2027. Both things are true.

sections.heading: The math no one wants to talk about
sections.body:
A workflow with ten steps, each ninety-five percent reliable, succeeds about sixty percent of the time. Five steps at the same rate gets you seventy-seven percent. This is Lusser's law, originally written for V-2 rockets, and it does not care that your steps are language models. Reliability multiplies down a chain.

The token bill follows the same logic in the wrong direction. Anthropic's own multi-agent research system—the one that beat single-Claude by ninety percent on internal research evals—burns about fifteen times the tokens of a single chat. Four times the tokens of a single agent. The gain is real and the eval is internal and the cost is the cost.

The literature on whether multi-agent debate actually helps is messier than the marketing. Some 2025 papers find debate decreases accuracy when one agent is more persuasive than correct. Others find vanilla debate underperforming simple majority voting at substantially higher cost. The honest read on six years of orchestration research: workflows materially outperform single calls when the task is parallelizable search, when an external verifier closes the loop, or when the agents are genuinely heterogeneous. Outside those three, the chassis is mostly cost.

sections.heading: The thirty-year inheritance
sections.body:
Here is the part the funding decks leave out. Almost every pattern the AI workflow ecosystem treats as novel has a published, named, taught ancestor between 1972 and 2014. The pattern is being re-derived from first principles, usually ad hoc, by engineers who have not read the literature.

The Workflow Management Coalition published their reference model in 1995. It cleanly separates the process definition, the enactment engine, the monitoring surface, the client APIs, and the interop layer into five distinct interfaces. This is what LangGraph and CrewAI are slowly rediscovering, one user complaint at a time. BPMN 2.0 made the diagrams and the runtime use the same artifact in 2011; most agent frameworks today ship one DSL for design and a different one for execution, which BPM corrected fifteen years ago.

Toyota's andon cord has been in print since 1978. Any worker pulls it, the line stops, the defect does not propagate. Most agent frameworks today let bad output flow straight into the next step. There is no andon cord; there is a retry counter and a prayer.

Pegasus has been doing provenance-capture and replay for LIGO since 2001. Snakemake has been pinning bioinformatics pipelines to exact Conda environments since 2012. You can replay a five-year-old LIGO analysis bit-for-bit. You cannot replay a five-week-old agent run, because nobody pinned the model version, the prompt, or the tool schema. That is a regression. We had this and we lost it.

Sagas—compensating transactions for long-running operations where you can't roll back the database—were specified by Garcia-Molina and Salem in 1987. Temporal and Inngest are rediscovering this in 2026. The name on the paper says SIGMOD '87. Most agent frameworks still have no concept of a named compensator for the email they sent, the card they charged, or the pull request they posted.

Van der Aalst's Workflow Patterns initiative documented forty-three control-flow patterns starting in 1999—parallel split, synchronizing merge, deferred choice, multi-instance with a priori known design-time knowledge, on and on. Score your agent DSL against the list and you will find it covers about ten and quietly fails on multi-instance synchronization. Parnas in 1972 told you to decompose around the secrets that may change, not around the steps in the process. Most agent decompositions today are step-by-step, which means they ossify the day the underlying model changes.

The HCI literature has the same shape. Horvitz wrote the rules for mixed-initiative interfaces in 1999: gate the interruption by uncertainty, not by step count. Lee and See in 2004: the goal is calibrated reliance, not maximum trust. Buçinca and colleagues showed in 2021 that AI explanations can make humans accept wrong AI more often—and in 2025 that contrastive framing ("you probably thought X; I chose Y because…") fixes it. Most workflow approval UIs in production today show a monologue, get a thumbs-up, and call it human-in-the-loop. It isn't. It's theater, and the recommender-systems literature has a name for that too.

sections.heading: What's actually new
sections.body:
Three things, and only three.

Step cost is stochastic. Tokens, latency, and quality vary per invocation. Every scheduler ever written assumes deterministic cost; agent workflows do not have that.

Inter-step contracts are interpreted, not validated. The step takes natural language and produces natural language. There is no type system at the boundary. Type-safety leaks. We are still working out what fills the gap—structured outputs, JSON schemas, validators—and the answer is "not yet."

The workflow rewrites itself at runtime. Adaptive replanning is real and load-bearing, and no classical workflow management system supports it because no classical workflow was supposed to think.

Everything else—provenance, compensators, halt authority, pattern coverage, modular decomposition, executable models, calibrated reliance, contrastive framing—is paid for in re-invention. The bill is the forty percent Gartner is forecasting.

sections.heading: Six moves for Monday
sections.body:
Pin the model, the prompt, and the tool schema per step. Treat it like a Conda env. If you cannot replay last week's run, you do not have a workflow, you have a séance.

Install andon authority at every step. Any step can halt the whole pipeline. The cost of stopping is dwarfed by the cost of bad output flowing into the next station.

Name your compensators. For every tool call that touches the world—sent email, charged card, posted PR, opened ticket—there is a compensating action with a name and an owner. If there isn't, that call should not be in the workflow.

Decompose by secrets, not by steps. Group everything that changes together into one agent; group everything stable together into another. When the model changes, the secret-hiding decomposition survives. The step-by-step decomposition does not.

Gate humans by uncertainty. Not every step. Not every five steps. The uncertainty cliff. And when you ask, frame contrastively: tell the human what you think they would have done, and why you chose differently. The literature is unanimous on this and the product surface is empty of it.

Never let the model be its own verifier. The verifier is a different model, ideally a different family, ideally with the original generator's reasoning hidden. Self-correction without an oracle does not work, and the field knows it does not work, and we ship products that pretend it does.

sections.heading: Looking ahead
sections.body:
Three things are going to happen in the next eighteen months, and they are not the things the LinkedIn cohort is excited about.

First, the workflow frameworks that survive will be the ones that read the BPM literature. Durable execution, named compensators, replayable provenance, pattern coverage—Temporal and Inngest already get this. The ones that don't will keep accreting features no one can debug.

Second, the verifier layer is going to become a separate product category. Not a feature of the workflow framework; a discrete thing you buy or build. The economics force it: if your chain has ten steps and you want any chance at production reliability, you need an oracle at each step, and the oracle cannot be the same model that produced the step. Watch this space.

Third, the human-in-the-loop UX is going to get rewritten from scratch. The current pattern—read AI output, click approve—is theater. The literature has the replacement (cognitive forcing, contrastive framing, uncertainty-gated interruption) and someone is going to ship it as a primitive. When they do, it's a category shift.

The workflow layer is here. It is not going anywhere. The companies that win this layer will be the ones that treated it as a re-emergence of a thirty-year discipline, not the invention of a new one. Everyone else pays the re-invention tax.

The literature is older than you think. Read it.
